paper_title: MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: While large language models (LLMs) show promise in scientific discovery, existing research focuses on inference or feedback-driven training, leaving the direct modeling of the generative reasoning process, $P(\text{hypothesis}|\text{background})$ ($P(h|b)$), unexplored. We demonstrate that directly training $P(h|b)$ is mathematically intractable due to the combinatorial complexity ($O(N^k)$) inherent in retrieving and composing inspirations from a vast knowledge base. To break this barrier, we introduce MOOSE-Star, a unified framework enabling tractable training and scalable inference. In the best case, MOOSE-Star reduces complexity from exponential to logarithmic ($O(\log N)$) by (1) training on decomposed subtasks derived from the probabilistic equation of discovery, (2) employing motivation-guided hierarchical search to enable logarithmic retrieval and prune irrelevant subspaces, and (3) utilizing bounded composition for robustness against retrieval noise. To facilitate this, we release TOMATO-Star, a dataset of 108,717 decomposed papers (38,400 GPU hours) for training. Furthermore, we show that while brute-force sampling hits a ''complexity wall,'' MOOSE-Star exhibits continuous test-time scaling.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 4 ] . [ 1 6 5 7 3 0 . 3 0 6 2 : r MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier Zonglin Yang 1 Lidong Bing 1 https://github.com/ZonglinY/MOOSE-Star https://huggingface.co/datasets/ZonglinY/TOMATO-Star https://huggingface.co/ZonglinY/MOOSE-Star-HC-R1D-7B https://huggingface.co/ZonglinY/MOOSE-Star-IR-R1D-7B Abstract While large language models (LLMs) show promise in scientific discovery, existing research focuses on inference or feedback-driven training, leaving the direct modeling of the generative reasoning process, (hypothesisbackground) (P (hb)), unexplored. We demonstrate that directly training (hb) is mathematically intractable due to the combinatorial complexity (O(N k)) inherent in retrieving and composing inspirations from vast knowledge base. To break this barrier, we introduce MOOSE-STAR, unified framework enabling tractable training and scalable inference. In the best case, MOOSESTAR reduces complexity from exponential to logarithmic (O(log )) by (1) training on decomposed subtasks derived from the probabilistic equation of discovery, (2) employing motivationguided hierarchical search to enable logarithmic retrieval and prune irrelevant subspaces, and (3) utilizing bounded composition for robustness against retrieval noise. To facilitate this, we release TOMATO-STAR, dataset of 108,717 decomposed papers (38,400 GPU hours) for training. Furthermore, we show that while brute-force sampling hits complexity wall, MOOSE-STAR exhibits continuous test-time scaling. 1. Introduction Recently, increasing research attention has been brought to the field of LLMs for scientific discovery. However, most research efforts to date focus on developing inference 1Infinity Lab, MiroMind AI. Correspondence to: Zonglin Lidong Bing <liYang <zonglin.yang@miromind.ai>, dong.bing@miromind.ai>. Preprint. March 5, 2026. 1 methodologies to leverage LLMs for discovery, with only few works investigating how to train an LLM specifically for this task (Luo et al., 2025). Critically, nearly all of these existing training approaches rely on external feedback to refine generated hypotheses, rather than explicitly modeling the core conditional probability (hypothesisbackground) (denoted as (hb)). In essence, these methods learn how to update hypothesis given feedback, but overlook the fundamental reasoning process required to generate highquality hypothesis directly from the research background. For example, Weng et al. (2024); Li et al. (2024) leverage open-access reviews to train critique models; Behzadifar et al. (2025) utilize alignment with observed data as reward; and Goel et al. (2025) employ rubric-based LLM feedback. Consequently, how to train (hb) remains unknown. In this paper, we provide theoretical analysis demonstrating why directly training (hb) is intractable due to its inherent combinatorial complexity. Furthermore, we propose recipe to overcome this barrier, enabling the tractable and, crucially, scalable training of (hb). To understand this intractability, we build upon the formalization established in prior studies (Yang et al., 2025b; Liu et al., 2025), viewing the generation of hypothesis as composition of the research background and sequence of latent inspirations retrieved from the global knowledge base. For instance, the discovery of Backpropagation can be viewed as composing the background of multilayer logistic regression with the inspiration of the chain rule from calculus. This compositional view is supported by cognitive science studies (Koestler, 1964; Benedek et al., 2012) and recent inference-based discovery methods (Yang et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024b; Garikaparthi et al., 2025; Kumar et al., 2025). However, directly training (hb) implies implicitly learning to retrieve the correct sequence of inspirations from literature space of size (e.g., 107, encoded within the LLMs pre-trained parameters). This results in combinatorial search space of O(N k), rendering end-to-end MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier training mathematically intractable. To break this complexity barrier, we introduce MOOSESTAR (MS), framework that transforms the intractable objective into solvable form through series of theoretical decompositions. Specifically, we leverage the mathematical formulation proposed by Yang et al. (2025b), which decomposes the combinatorial complexity of (hb) into set of subtasks with linear complexity: retrieving inspirations (sequentially) and composing each inspiration into hypothesis. Crucially, while Yang et al. (2025b) utilized this equation solely for inference, we operationalize it here for training. This decouples the exponential O(N k) dependency into independent sequential steps, reducing the sample complexity to linear O(k ). However, even with this decomposition, explicitly scanning the full literature (N ) remains computationally prohibitive. To address this, we introduce three key innovations: (1) Hierarchical Search: We organize the global literature into semantic search tree, replacing the expensive linear scan with top-down navigation. This reduces the retrieval complexity from linear O(N ) to logarithmic O(log ) in (2) Bounded Composition: To the best-case scenario. handle retrieval imperfections, we introduce semantic tolerance radius. By training the composition module on noisy inspirations within bounded window centered on the exact inspiration i, we ensure the model is robust to inexact retrievals. (3) Motivation Planning: We demonstrate that explicitly modeling Motivation variableappended to the research backgroundacts as dynamic generative root for the search tree. By effectively pruning irrelevant branches during hierarchical search, this reduces the number of search steps required to locate the relevant semantic subspace (Nm < ). To support this data-hungry training paradigm, we developed comprehensive pipeline that processes scientific literature across domains such as biology, chemistry, and cognitive science. In total, we extracted research backgrounds, ground-truth hypotheses, and inspirationseach explicitly linked to historical citationfrom 108,717 papers, massive computational effort consuming approximately 38,400 A800 GPU hours. We release this processed dataset as the TOMATO-STAR dataset. Finally, we demonstrate in 7 that MOOSE-STAR achieves superior test-time scaling compared to brute-force sampling. While brute-force sampling hits complexity wall on hypotheses requiring the composition of multiple inspirations, our inspiration-based hierarchical search exhibits continuous improvement in success rate with increased inference budget, effectively turning an intractable discovery problem into manageable search process. Our contributions can be summarized as below: We establish the first theoretical analysis on why training (hb) is intractable (combinatorial complexity). We provide the first training recipe on how to enable the tractable and scalable training of (hb). We provide an inference recipe that supports more scalable test-time inference for discovery. We release dataset of 108,717 processed papers (cost 38,400 GPU hours), along with the full training & inference codebase and trained models. 2. Related Work While significant body of research focuses on novel inference algorithms for LLM-based discovery (Romera-Paredes et al., 2024; Novikov et al., 2025; Shojaee et al., 2025; Lu et al., 2024), few studies investigate methods for training LLMs for discovery. Most existing training efforts focus on constructing external feedback mechanisms for generated hypotheses rather than modeling the core conditional probability (hb) itself. For instance, Weng et al. (2024); Li et al. (2024) leverage peer review data to train critique models, which serve as reward signals for the discovery agent. Similarly, in the domain of equation discovery, Behzadifar et al. (2025) derive rewards based on the goodness of fit between the proposed hypothesis (equation) and observed data. Goel et al. (2025) extract evaluation criteria from scientific literature to construct rubric-based LLM self-evaluators, while Pu et al. (2025) train models to retrieve ancillary high-level guidance from constrained set to assist discovery. ONeill et al. (2025) attempt to model (hb) directly; however, obtaining the reasoning trace towards from only via distillation is prohibitive (as discussed in 6), and generating simulated reasoning traces directly from and the ground truth as input is very difficult to work (Wang et al., 2025). This work focuses exclusively on training LLMs for scientific discovery, distinct from related fields such as general scientific reasoning (Bai et al., 2025) or modeling of specific science modality (Jumper et al., 2021). 3. Preliminary: The Decomposition Theory Our framework builds upon the probabilistic decomposition theory introduced in MOOSE-Chem (Yang et al., 2025b). Fundamentally, this theory posits that scientific hypothesis is not generated ex nihilo (out of nothing) but is synthesized by composing research background with sequence of latent inspirations, = (i1, . . . , ik), retrieved from global knowledge base (N = I). Formally, the generation of hypothesis can be modeled as: = (b, i1, . . . , ik) (1) 2 MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier This compositional view is well-supported by cognitive science literature (Koestler, 1964) and has proven empirically effective across scientific disciplines (Liu et al., 2025). For instance, the discovery of Backpropagation can be viewed as composing the background of multilayer logistic regression with the inspiration of the chain rule from calculus; similarly, new synthesis method in chemistry might be derived by applying specific catalyst (inspiration) to known reaction mechanism. Based on this formulation, we can approximate the intractable marginal likelihood (h b) by decomposing the generation process into sequential steps. Assuming Markov property where the intermediate hypothesis hj1 sufficiently encapsulates the history of prior inspirations, the probability can be factorized using the chain rule as: (h b) (cid:89) j=1 (ij b, hj1, I) (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:124) Inspiration Retrieval (hj b, hj1, ij) (cid:124) (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) Hypothesis Composition (2) where hj denotes the intermediate hypothesis state after the j-th step (with h0 = and hk = h). The first term represents the retrieval of the next relevant inspiration, and the second term represents the composition of the hypothesis using the retrieved inspiration. 4. Dataset Construction To enable the training of our framework, we construct TOMATO-STAR, large-scale dataset of scientific papers processed into structured components. The construction pipeline consists of four stages: collection, decomposition, structured representation, and quality assurance. Data Collection and Split. We collected 108,717 openaccess papers from the NCBI database (Schoch et al., 2020), spanning biology, chemistry, and cognitive science. The corpus covers the period from January 2020 to October 2025. To rigorously evaluate generalization, we enforce strict temporal split: papers published from January 2020 to September 2025 constitute the training set, while papers from October 2025 serve as the held-out test set. We also perform explicit checking to make sure the two sets do not have overlap with each other. October 2025 is latest enough, so that the evaluation results should not be contaminated. Preprocessing and Decomposition. Raw PDF documents are first converted to Markdown format using MinerU (Wang et al., 2024a). We then employ pipeline of locally deployed reasoning models, specifically DeepSeekR1 and R1-distilled-Qwen-32b (Guo et al., 2025), to decompose each paper into tuple of (b, h, i): Research Background, Hypothesis, and Inspirations. This workflow adapts the design of ResearchBench (Liu et al., 2025) but introduces stricter quality controls and novel hypothesis representation scheme. Structured Representation. The decomposed components are defined as follows: Research Background (b): Comprises the specific research question and background survey summarizing prior methods addressing that question. Inspirations (i): Ground-truth inspirations are identified directly from the source papers citations. We augment these citations by retrieving their full titles and abstracts via Semantic Scholar (Kinney et al., 2023). Hypothesis (h) as Incremental Deltas: Unlike standard summaries, we structure the hypothesis as sequence of Delta Hypotheses (h), enforcing oneto-one mapping where each inspiration ij results in exactly one hj. The final hypothesis is the concatenation of these deltas. Crucially, each is structured into three levels: (1) Motivation (why this direction was chosen), (2) Mechanism (why it can work), and (3) Methodology (how it is implemented). Quality Assurance. To ensure data integrity, every processed sample must pass four automated quality checks prior to inclusion: (1) Information Necessity: Each proposed must provide essential, complementary knowledge required to derive from b. (2) Information Sufficiency: The integration of the background and inspirations must logically entail the hypothesis (b + h). (3) Information Disjointness: The background must remain strictly independent, leaking no information present in either the inspirations or the hypothesis. (4) Non-Redundancy: The extracted inspirations within must be mutually distinct and non-repetitive. 5. Methodology In this section, we first analyze why directly training (hb) is computationally intractable. We then derive the MOOSESTAR framework, showing how each component progressively transforms the intractable objective into solvable problem with manageable complexity. 5.1. Analysis: The Intractability Barrier (O(N k)) Consider the standard baseline approach: training model to directly maximize the marginal likelihood (h b) end-toend. As established in Eq. 1, generating valid hypothesis inherently requires identifying and synthesizing sequence of inspirations {i1, . . . , ik}. Consequently, direct modeling (h b) implies an implicit search over the Cartesian product of the knowledge base, k. Even disregarding the computational cost of composition, the search space com3 MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier plexity alone scales exponentially: CE2E > O(N k) (e.g., for = 107, = 3 : 1021) (3) Here, = 107 represents the scale of global scientific literature, where each paper constitutes an independent unit of knowledge. Crucially, this complexity barrier persists even in the absence of an external retriever. When relying solely on internal weights, the LLM is forced to implicitly retrieve from the knowledge encoded during pretraining. Given this intractable O(N k) search space, standard end-to-end training becomes mathematically ill-posed, resulting in severe convergence difficulties. 5.2. Method I: Decomposed Sequential Training (O(N )) To dismantle the exponential barrier of O(N k), our first methodology shifts from directly training the monolithic (h b) to translating the theoretical decomposition in Eq. 2 into concrete training objective. We train the model on two equivalent sequential subtasks: Inspiration Retrieval and Hypothesis Composition. For each step j, the complexity breakdown is as follows: Inspiration Retrieval (IR): The task is to identify the correct ground-truth inspiration from the full global knowledge base. This requires scanning the search space of size , resulting in complexity of O(N ). Hypothesis Composition (HC): The task is to generate hj given the retrieved inspiration ij. Conditioned on the provision of the exact ground-truth inspiration, the generation in the ideal case requires only single inference pass. Thus, relative to the search space size , this step has constant complexity of O(1). Since this process is repeated sequentially for steps, this methodology fundamentally transforms the search space topology from Cartesian product (N k) to linear sum (k ). Consequently, the complexity is: (cid:104) CI O(N ) (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) Inspiration Retrieval + O(1) (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) Hypothesis Composition (cid:105) (4) We formulate both IR and HC as generative reasoning tasks, requiring the model to produce Chain-of-Thought (CoT) (Wei et al., 2022) rationale before outputting the final answer. To optimize this reasoning capability, we adopt teacher-based Rejection Sampling Fine-Tuning (RFT) (Touvron et al., 2023; Zelikman et al., 2022). Specifically, we utilize R1-DISTILLED-QWEN-32B as the teacher model to generate training samples, which are then filtered to finetune the student model, R1-DISTILLED-QWEN-7B. For the IR task, the model is trained to identify the target inspiration via generative selection process. The input consists of the query context and candidate pool of 15 papers (1 positive and 14 negatives), each represented by its title and abstract. The negative set comprises easy negatives (randomly sampled papers published no later than the source paper) and hard negatives drawn from three specific categories: papers with keyword overlap with the source background, papers with keyword overlap with the ground-truth inspiration, and papers semantically similar to the ground-truth inspiration. We acknowledge that these negatives might occasionally indicate valid alternative inspirations. However, the ground-truth citation represents the authors explicit acknowledgment of causal influence, providing the most reliable supervision signal for prioritizing optimal inspirations over merely related ones. Furthermore, this single-selection objective minimizes the penalty for potentially valid unlabelled candidates compared to independently labeling each candidate. For the HC task, the objective is to generate the incremental hypothesis update, hj. The model is conditioned on the research background b, the retrieved ground-truth inspiration ij, and hj1, represented by the concatenation of {h0, . . . , hj1} (if 1). To enable effective rejection sampling, we developed rubric-based evaluator to compare the quality of sampled hypotheses against the ground-truth hj, ensuring that only high-quality samples are retained for training. The evaluation rubric is provided in C. 5.3. Method II: Bounded Composition While the linear decomposition O(kN ) breaks the exponential barrier, linearly scanning 107 papers for every retrieval step remains computationally prohibitive for testtime inference. To address this, we must further optimize the linear term O(N ). We achieve this by relaxing the retrieval objective. In 5.2, the formulation assumes strict Exact Match constraint: the IR module must identify the unique ground-truth inspiration (1 out of ), and the HC module relies solely on this exact input. We propose Bounded Composition, which introduces Semantic Tolerance Space of size . Instead of requiring i, we posit that the HC module can be trained to function robustly within semantic neighborhood Ii centered on containing candidates (where Ii I). If the retriever provides any inspiration Ii , the HC module is expected to reason through this proxy to recover the underlying and correctly compose the hypothesis. This relaxation alters the complexity landscape: IR Relaxation (O(N ) O(N/M )): The retrieval target expands from single point to subspace of size 4 MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier . In randomized search or sampling process, the expected number of steps to hit target of size in space of reduces linearly to N/M . strategy explicitly maximizes the learned semantic tolerance space, forcing the model to reason robustly even with less precise retrieval contexts. HC Overhead (O(1) O(M )): The composition cost increases. The HC model must now perform additional reasoning to resolve the ambiguity within the tolerance space. We represent this abstractly as an internal complexity of O(M ), reflecting the cost of reasoning within the local space of possibilities. Consequently, the overall complexity updates to: C{I,II} (cid:104) (cid:19) (cid:18) (cid:123)(cid:122) Relaxed Retrieval (cid:124) (cid:125) + O(M ) (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) Bounded Composition (cid:105) (5) This trade-off is strategic: while HC complexity increases linearly with , the dominant IR complexity decreases by factor of . Given that , shifting the computational burden from the global search space to local reasoning is significant net reduction in total complexity. Figure 1. Bounded Composition. The concentric circles around represent similarity thresholds that define the bounded space . To operationalize Bounded Composition, we first curate pool of semantic proxies. For each ground-truth inspiration in the dataset, we query Semantic Scholar to retrieve up to 50 semantically similar papers. We embed these candidates using SPECTER2 (Singh et al., 2022) and compute their cosine similarity to i. Based on these scores, we stratify valid candidates into three difficulty tiers: Easy ([0.94, 0.97)), Medium ([0.92, 0.94)), and Hard ([0.90, 0.92)). From each available tier, we select the single candidate with the highest similarity score to serve as the tier representative. We then employ the teacher model to generate hypotheses conditioned on these proxy inspirations rather than the exact ground truth. The generated outputs are rigorously evaluated against the quality control rubric described in C. Crucially, for instances where proxies from multiple tiers yield passing hypotheses, we prioritize the sample from the most difficult tier (i.e., lowest similarity) for the final training set. This 5 5.4. Method III: Hierarchical Search (O(log )) Despite the reduction to O(N/M ), linear scan over the global knowledge base remains inefficient. To enable logarithmic-time access (at least in the best-case scenario), we propose replacing the flat scan with Hierarchical BestFirst Search. This design draws inspiration from MOOSECHEM2 (Yang et al., 2025a), which formulates fine-grained hypothesis discovery as hierarchical search over varying levels of detail abstraction. Here, we adapt this concept by constructing the hierarchy directly over the inspiration space I. By organizing into semantic search tree, we can rapidly navigate to the most promising subspaces, pruning irrelevant branches early in the process. Tree Construction (Offline). We construct the search tree via bottom-up recursive clustering approach. First, all papers in are embedded using SPECTER2. We then perform hierarchical K-means clustering (McQueen, 1967) recursively from the leaves up to the root. In each iteration, we cluster the current levels embeddings (initially the papers, subsequently the cluster centroids), where the resulting centroids serve as the nodes for the next higher level of the hierarchy. This process repeats until single root centroid remains. Crucially, to align with our IR models capacity, we enforce maximum branching factor of = 15. We employ post-clustering balancing operation: if cluster exceeds size c, its furthest member (least similar to the centroid) is reassigned to its nearest centroid in the same hierarchy that has available capacity (size < c). This ensures the tree is balanced with consistent branching factor. Best-First Search (Online). During inference, we traverse the tree using Best-First Search (BFS) strategy. We maintain priority queue of candidate nodes, ranked by path confidence score. In each iteration, we pop the highest-scoring node for expansion. The IR model evaluates the sub-branches of this node, generating probability distribution over its children (derived from the output token log-probabilities). Crucially, these local probabilities are aggregated with the parent nodes stored cumulative probability to compute updated scores for each child, which are then pushed back into the priority queue. To ensure fair comparison between nodes at different depthsavoiding the bias against deeper paths inherent in raw cumulative productswe define the ranking score as the geometric mean of the probabilities along the path from the root: (cid:118) (cid:117) (cid:117) Score(pathj) = j+1 (cid:116) (cid:89) pt t= (6) MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier where pt is the probability assigned by the IR model at depth t. By dynamically updating the search frontier based on this length-normalized metric, the algorithm effectively prioritizes the most promising semantic neighborhoods without traversing the entire database. In the best-case scenario (where the IR model makes ideal routing decisions), the search complexity corresponds to the depth of the tree, which is logarithmic with respect to the search space. This yields more tractable complexity: C{I,II,III} (cid:104) (cid:18) log (cid:19)

sections.content: (cid:124) (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) Hierarchical Search + O(M ) (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) Bounded Composition (cid:105) (7) 5.5. Method IV: Motivation Planning While hierarchical search optimizes the traversal of the knowledge base, it is essentially an unguided navigation: the search process often lacks explicit direction, essentially stumbling into candidates without prior intent. To address this, we introduce Motivation variable (m). Semantic Guidance. Functionally, serves as Directional Guide generated prior to retrieval. By appending to the query context b, we explicitly condition the hierarchical search on specific intended properties or requirements. This biases the search focus towards relevant semantic manifolds, filtering out branches inconsistent with the high-level intent. Consequently, the effective search space is reduced from the global knowledge base to motivation-aligned subspace Nm (where Nm < ). O(1) Generation. Crucially, the motivation is derived solely from the research background via internal abstraction (e.g., single-step translation of background information into high-level requirements). We intentionally design this planning step to be lightweightapproximately O(1) (a single inference)to ensure it neither becomes computational bottleneck nor generates overly specific directives that could misguide the subsequent computationally expensive IR and HC resources. Assuming the generated motivation is sound (a reasonable assumption for this lightweight translation step), the overall complexity is further reduced to: CMS (cid:104) O(1) (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) Motivation (cid:18) + log (cid:19) Nm (cid:124) (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) Focused Retrieval (cid:105) + O(M ) (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) Bounded Composition (8) Hierarchical MDP Formulation. This addition formally extends the decomposition in Eq. 2 into tri-stage Hierarchical Markov Decision Process (HMDP). At each constructive iteration j, the model first formulates high-level intent mj before executing retrieval and composition: (h b) (cid:89) (cid:104) j=1 (mj b, hj1) (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:124) Motivation Planning (ij b, hj1, mj, I) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) (cid:124) Inspiration Retrieval (hj b, hj1, mj, ij) (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:124) Hypothesis Composition (cid:105) (9) The formal derivation of Eq. 9 is provided in B. Compared to the base formulation, the additional term (mj b, hj1) explicitly governs the motivation planning step, ensuring that the subsequent retrieval (ij) and composition (hj) remain aligned with coherent research trajectory. 6. Experiment In this section, we empirically evaluate the four methodologies proposed in 5. All experiments utilize the TOMATOSTAR test set, comprising papers published in October 2025, to ensure rigorous evaluation free from contamination. 6.1. Method I: Decomposed Sequential Training Table 1. Inspiration retrieval results. Model Random Selection R1-DISTILLED-QWEN-7B MS-IR-7B Accuracy 6.70% 28.42% 54.37% Table 2. Hypothesis composition performance given the groundtruth inspiration i. Total aggregates the scores for Motivation (Mot), Mechanism (Mec), and Methodology (Met). w/ bounded indicates the model was trained with additional copies of bounded composition data. Model Total Mot Mec Met Length R1-DISTILLED-QWEN-7B MS-HC-7B w/ 1 bounded w/ 2 bounded 4.34 5.08 5.16 5.15 1.96 2.21 2.24 2. 1.40 1.58 1.60 1.58 0.97 1.29 1.31 1.32 231.02 204.12 203.84 205. (IR). We Inspiration Retrieval fine-tuned R1DISTILLED-QWEN-7B on dataset of 152k retrieval examples, resulting in MS-IR-7B (where MS stands for MOOSE-STAR). As shown in Table 1, this specialized training yields substantial performance gain, boosting accuracy from 28.42% to 54.37%. 6 MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier Hypothesis Composition (HC). We fine-tuned R1DISTILLED-QWEN-7B on 97k samples generated via teacher-based rejection sampling, resulting in MS-HC-7B (MS stands for MOOSE-STAR). Performance is evaluated using the M3 score (detailed in C), an LLM-based rubric summing scores for Motivation, Mechanism, and Methodology. Each dimension ranges from [0, 4] (representing 0%100% information recall), yielding total score of 12. Table 2 presents the results on ground-truth inputs. The finetuned MS-HC-7B significantly outperforms the baseline. Furthermore, results indicate that incorporating bounded training data (hypotheses generated from noisy inspirations) further enhances performance, suggesting that exposure to semantic variations during training improves reasoning robustness even when evaluating on perfect inspirations. 6.2. Method II: Bounded Composition Table 3 evaluates performance under noisy conditions, using proxy inspirations stratified by semantic similarity to (Easy, Medium, Hard). The results demonstrate that incorporating bounded training data significantly boosts M3 scores across all tiers compared to the base model. 6.3. Method III: Hierarchical Search We evaluate search efficiency on corpus of 3,035 groundtruth inspirations derived from the 1,658 test papers (October 2025). hierarchical tree is constructed over this corpus as described in 5.4. Table 4 compares our Hierarchical Best-First Search against Tournament Search baseline. The baseline employs an exhaustive bottom-up strategy, processing all candidates through the cluster structure to identify winners, which incurs high, fixed computational cost. We report two metrics: IR Inference Calls (the average number of IR queries required to successfully locate i) and Proposed Rank (the average position of in the retrieval order, where lower is better). The results demonstrate that our hierarchical approach is significantly more efficient, reducing inference calls by approximately 3 (67.78 vs. 218.00) while achieving superior (lower) average rank. This confirms that the topdown, probability-guided search effectively prunes irrelevant branches without compromising retrieval accuracy. 6.4. Method IV: Motivation Planning We analyze the impact of motivation quality on search efficiency by utilizing ground-truth derived directives. We compare two variants: (1) Simple: direct translation of requirements from with minimal reasoning and specification. (2) Detailed: Derived from the delta hypothesis, which we observe typically captures strategic intent without leaking specific mechanism or methodology details. As shown in Table 4, incorporating motivation consistently improves search efficiency over the baseline. The detailed variant achieves the best performance, confirming that more detailed and precise planning can reduce more computational effort required to locate i. 7. Scaling Analysis 7.1. Scaling Brute-force Sampling (hb) Direct distillation from the end-to-end distribution (hb) is intractable due to the extreme scarcity of valid reasoning traces. Table 5 illustrates this bottleneck: finding qualified hypothesis is rare even for simple cases (k = 1), and becomes statistically impossible for multi-inspiration trajectories (k 2) due to combinatorial explosion. In contrast, the decomposed HC step demonstrates high success rate (47.33%). This performance gain is driven by two structural advantages. Primarily, by isolating single inspiration step, the HC task circumvents the combinatorial complexity of the full problem: it is only required to solve for one incremental innovation hj rather than the joint distribution of all innovations. Secondarily, the decomposition outsources the search burden to the IR module; thus, conditioning on the provided inspiration explicitly guides the generation. Together, these factors empirically validate the complexity analysis in 5. Evaluation Standard and The Training Deadlock. To understand why this performance gap determines training feasibility, we must examine the criteria used for harvesting RFT training data (detailed in C). Both strategies use the same rubric, evaluating generated hypothesis component against the ground truth across Motivation, Mechanism, and Methodology. To serve as positive sample for RFT, we impose filtering threshold: the generation must achieve Total Score 8 (out of 12). The structural difference lies in how this threshold applies to the two paradigms: Brute-force: Since the baseline generates the hypothesis holistically, valid sample for paper with 2 must successfully meet the filtering threshold for every constituent innovation (j = 1 . . . k) simultaneously. This strict conjunctive constraint drives the success rate to near-zero, creating training deadlock: the model cannot generate enough valid samples to initiate RFT, rendering the training process impossible. HC: In contrast, the HC module is trained to synthesize single increment hj. Crucially, leveraging the decomposed data construction, we can condition the model on the ground-truth inspiration . Under this setup, the model only needs to meet the threshold for 7 MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier Table 3. Hypothesis composition performance under varying levels of inspiration noise. The tiers Easy (E), Medium (M), and Hard (H) correspond to the semantic similarity of the retrieved proxy inspiration to the ground truth. w/ bounded indicates the model was trained with additional copies of bounded composition data. Bold values denote the best performance in each column. Easy (E) Medium (M) Hard (H) Model Total Mot Mec Met Total Mot Mec Met Total Mot Mec Met R1-DISTILLED-QWEN-7B MS-HC-7B w/ 1 bounded w/ 2 bounded 2.58 2.82 2.96 3.03 1. 1.53 1.55 1.62 0.62 0.62 0.70 0.69 0.54 0.67 0.71 0.72 2. 2.37 2.52 2.49 1.24 1.36 1.41 1.37 0.46 0.47 0.53 0.53 0. 0.55 0.58 0.59 1.82 2.20 2.19 2.25 1.13 1.30 1.25 1.29 0. 0.40 0.44 0.44 0.36 0.49 0.49 0.52 Table 4. Efficiency and accuracy of hierarchical search variants compared to tournament baseline. IR inference calls: Average number of IR model queries required to identify the ground-truth inspiration. Proposed Rank: The average position of the groundtruth inspiration in the retrieved list (lower is better). Search Method IR inference calls Proposed Rank Tournament Search Hierarchical Search w/ motivation (simple) w/ motivation (detailed) 218.00 67.78 63.80 63.05 987. 813.40 767.64 742.50 Table 5. Comparison of sampling pass rates between Brute-force (End-to-End) and Hypothesis Composition (Decomposed). While Brute-force performance collapses to 0.00% as complexity (k) increases, the decomposed HC module maintains robust 47.33% pass rate, unlocking the data engine for RFT. Method Steps (k) Total Samples Passed Samples Pass Rate Brute-force (hb) 1 2 3 Total HC (hi, b) 1 (per step) 31,314 67,000 12,000 110, 195,301 653 90 0 743 2.09% 0.13% 0.00% 0.67% 92, 47.33% the corresponding single . Consequently, this efficient sampling capability breaks the training deadlock, making it feasible to train HC using RFT. 7.2. Scaling Training of MOOSE-STAR We analyze the scaling behavior of the IR and HC models with training data ranging from 100 to 105 samples (Figure 2a and 2b). The IR model demonstrates standard log-linear improvement in accuracy. In contrast, the HC model exhibits distinct threshold behavior: significant loglinear performance gains emerge only after the dataset size exceeds 103 samples. One potential reason for this disparity is that IR is fundamentally classification task, whereas HC is more complex generative task requiring higher data density to capture the nuances of scientific synthesis. Scalability of Decomposition. These results are pivotal as they validate the training paradigm established in Equation 2. They demonstrate that the decomposed subtasksInspiration Retrieval (IR) and Hypothesis Composition (HC)accomplish more than simply converting the intractable problem of scientific discovery into tractable, learnable sub-problems; they also establish scalable training paradigm that yields log-linear performance improvements with increasing data scale. (a) Inspiration retrieval. (b) Hypothesis composition. Figure 2. Scaling laws of MOOSE-STAR: (a) IR and (b) HC. Generalization on OOD Retrieval. The continuous scaling of the IR task is particularly noteworthy and counterintuitive. Prior literature typically characterizes scientific inspiration retrieval as an Out-of-Distribution (OOD) task (Yang et al., 2025b), given that true discovery requires associating concepts that have never been linked in the prior 8 MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier (a) Cumulative success vs inference steps. (b) Win-rate by problem complexity. Figure 3. Test-time scaling behavior of MOOSE-STAR versus brute-force. knowledge base. Despite this inherent novelty barrier, the consistent log-linear improvement suggests that the model is not merely memorizing seen connections, but is potentially acquiring generalizable logic of discoveryan abstract intuition for identifying meaningful scientific associations across previously unidentified knowledge connections. 7.3. Test-time Scaling of MOOSE-STAR Figure 3a compares the test-time scaling efficiency of MOOSE-STAR against the brute-force baseline. The evaluation is conducted on subset of TOMATO-STAR comprising the first 109 test cases. This sample size corresponds to the first 200 sequential inspiration steps in the dataset, as each research paper in our corpus typically involves composition of [1, 3] inspirations. The x-axis represents the computational cost measured in cumulative inference calls. For MOOSE-STAR, this total sums (1) all IR inference calls required to navigate the hierarchical search tree, and (2) the number of HC inference calls equivalent to the proposed rank of the groundtruth inspiration. Specifically, for an inspiration proposed at rank n, we account for separate HC composition attempts: every retrieved inspiration candidate usually needs HC model inference to compose hypothesis. For the baseline, the cost simply equals the total number of independent samples generated. The y-axis tracks the success rate, defined as the proportion of cases where generated hypothesis matches or exceeds the quality of the MOOSE-STAR final output, judged by how well it captures the innovative essence of h. We utilize the MOOSE-STAR output as the reference standard for two main reasons: (1) Direct side-by-side comparison is the most robust method for determining relative superiority between two approaches; (2) Since MOOSE-STAR is conditioned on the complete set of ground-truth inspirations (i), it inherently serves as high-quality proxy for the ground truth, setting strong baseline for validating whether unguided methods can recover the key scientific concepts. We implement the pairwise comparison with Gemini-3-Flash (Google DeepMind, 2025). To mitigate the influence of position bias and inference variance, each pair is evaluated four times (across two independent trials, each including both original and swapped positions). The results reveal stark contrast in scaling behavior. MOOSE-STAR achieves steady progress, continuously solving more complex cases as the search budget increases, eventually reaching 100% coverage of the test set at 6,000 inference calls. Conversely, the brute-force baseline saturates at 41.3%. Figure 3b provides breakdown of this saturation, revealing the steep complexity cliff where unstructured sampling fails. For MOOSE-STAR, each HC inference is effectively seeded by specific retrieved inspiration i, allowing for diverse and directed exploration of the hypothesis space. In contrast, the baseline relies on unguided sampling. The impact of this difference is profound: even when fully penalized for search costs (by accounting for proposed rank in HC inference calls), MOOSE-STAR essentially relies on single guided hypothesis sample (conditioned on the retrieved i) to compete against massive barrage of 9,500 independent brute-force samples. On simple, single-step tasks (k = 1), the baseline leverages this sheer volume to achieve 53% success rate. However, this volume advantage is rapidly negated by the combinatorial explosion. As the reasoning chain extends, the baselines performance collapsesplummeting to 36% for = 2 and further evaporating to negligible 8% for = 3. This dramatic decay empirically confirms that without the structural guidance of decomposition, even nearly ten thousand unguided attempts are statistically insufficient to break 9 MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier the combinatorial complexity barrier. 8. Conclusion We address the intractability of training LLMs for scientific discovery by demonstrating that end-to-end modeling of (hb) is ill-posed due to combinatorial complexity. To overcome this, we introduce MOOSE-STAR, which operationalizes the probabilistic decomposition proposed in MOOSE-CHEM to enable tractable training. Our results confirm that the synergy of Hierarchical Search, Bounded Composition, and Motivation Planning reduces search complexity from exponential to logarithmic in the best-case scenario. Crucially, while brute-force methods hit complexity wall on hypotheses requiring multiple inspirations, MOOSE-STAR exhibits continuous test-time scaling, transforming discovery into manageable search process. We release TOMATO-STAR to facilitate future research.

Advances in neural information processing systems, 35:2482424837, 2022. 11 MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier A. MOOSE-Star Concept Figure Figure 4. MOOSE-Star Concept Figure B. Theoretical Derivation: Rigorous Decomposition of (h b) via Hierarchical MDP B.1. Extended Fundamental Assumption Building upon the foundational assumption in (Yang et al., 2025b) that scientific hypotheses originate from background knowledge and inspirations, we introduce the concept of Motivation (m). We posit that the discovery process is not merely selection of entities, but hierarchical decision process: before identifying specific inspiration (e.g., specific paper or entity), researcher first formulates high-level strategic intent or motivation (e.g., methodology category or research direction). We translate this extended assumption into the following functional form: = (b, (m1, i1), . . . , (mk, ik)) (10) Here, mj serves as the semantic anchor or abstract strategy for the j-th step, and ij is the concrete inspiration instantiation conditioned on mj. The tuple (mj, ij) represents composite unit of inspiration for each step. Consistent with the previous theoretical framework (Yang et al., 2025b), we maintain two critical assumptions to ensure mathematical tractability by collapsing the otherwise intractable marginalization space: 1. Uniqueness Assumption: Each valid hypothesis corresponds to unique minimal set of motivation-inspiration pairs, {(m1, i1), . . . , (mk, ik)}. Motivation: Instead of assuming hypothesis is an arbitrary, intractable amalgamation of global knowledge, this posits that discovery is driven by specific, identifiable set of core insights. This allows us to concentrate the probability mass entirely on this single optimal set of ingredients. 2. Fixed-Order Assumption: The integration of this set follows canonical constructive sequence (e.g., chronologically or by logical dependency). Motivation: In empirical sciences, the order in which inspirations are integrated can often be interchangeable. However, adopting fixed canonical order serves to simplify the mathematical exposition by eliminating the need to marginalize over all k! possible permutations of the selected pairs, effectively reducing the 12 MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier generation process to single, deterministic reasoning path. (We provide the generalized formulation relaxing this assumption at the end of this section). B.2. Hierarchical Markov Decision Process Formulation We formalize the hypothesis generation process as Hierarchical Markov Decision Process (Hierarchical MDP). Unlike standard MDP where the action is single step, we define the decision at step as hierarchical process involving high-level planner and low-level executor: State: sj1 = (b, hj1), representing the background context and the intermediate hypothesis generated so far (with h0 = ). High-level Action (Motivation): mj M, selected by high-level policy πhigh. This action abstracts the search space. Low-level Action (Inspiration): ij I, selected by low-level policy πlow. This action is conditioned on mj. Transition (Composition): The environment updates the state to sj = (b, hj) by integrating the selected inspiration ij into the previous intermediate hypothesis hj1 under the guidance of the motivation mj. B.3. Derivation of the Decomposition Our goal is to decompose the intractable marginal likelihood (h b). Let denote the deterministic global knowledge base. Since it serves as fixed, omnipresent context for the discovery process, conditioning on the background is practically equivalent to conditioning on the joint context (b, I). Under the uniqueness and fixed-order assumptions, the marginalization over all possible latent variables collapses to the joint probability of the unique constructive path defined by the sequence {(m1, i1), . . . , (mk, ik)}: (h b) = (m1, i1, . . . , mk, ik, h1, . . . , hk b) = (cid:89) j=1 (cid:89) j=1 (mj, ij, hj b, m1, i1, h1, . . . , mj1, ij1, hj1) (mj, ij, hj b, hj1, I) (11) (12) (13) Equation 11 establishes the joint distribution of the unique path. Equation 12 applies the standard chain rule over the time steps = 1 . . . k, explicitly capturing the full historical trajectory up to step 1. Equation 13 applies the Markov property, assuming the decision at step depends primarily on the research background b, the global knowledge base (introduced here as the explicit search space), and the immediate previous hypothesis state hj1. Intra-step Decomposition. Now, we perform the decomposition for the joint term at single step j, (mj, ij, hj b, hj1, I). We first apply the exact chain rule and then apply conditional independence assumptions inherent to the scientific discovery process: (mj, ij, hj b, hj1, I) = (mj b, hj1, I) (ij b, hj1, mj, I) (hj b, hj1, mj, ij, I) (mj b, hj1) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) Step 1: Planning (hj b, hj1, mj, ij) (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:124) Step 3: Composition (ij b, hj1, mj, I) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) (cid:124) Step 2: Retrieval (cid:124) (14) (15) The transition from Equation 14 to 15 is justified by two independence properties: 13 MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier 1. Internal Planning Independence: The strategic motivation mj is an internal cognitive state generated by the models reasoning over the context (b, hj1). It is conditionally independent of the external database given the context (P (m . . . , I) (m . . . )). 2. Local Composition Independence: Once specific inspiration ij is retrieved, the formation of the next hypothesis state hj relies on the semantic content of ij and the current context. The rest of the global database becomes redundant (P (h . . . , ij, I) (h . . . , ij)). Substituting Equation 15 back into Equation 13, we obtain the final tractable objective: (h b) (cid:89) (cid:104) j= (mj b, hj1) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) (cid:124) Motivation Planning (ij b, hj1, mj, I) (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:124) Inspiration Retrieval (hj b, hj1, mj, ij) (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:124) Bounded Composition (cid:105) (16) Generalization without the Fixed-Order Assumption. While the fixed-order assumption significantly simplifies the theoretical exposition, in practice, the components of scientific hypothesis can often be assembled in various valid sequences. Relaxing this assumption requires marginalizing over all valid permutations Πk of the steps. The generalized decomposition for MOOSE-STAR is thus: (h b) = (cid:88) πΠk (mπ(1), iπ(1), . . . , mπ(k), iπ(k), h(π) 1 , . . . , h(π) b) (cid:88) (cid:89) (cid:104) πΠk j=1 (mπ(j) b, h(π) j1) (iπ(j) b, h(π) j1, mπ(j), I) (h(π) b, h(π) (cid:105) j1, mπ(j), iπ(j)) (17) where h(π) 0 = , h(π) = h, and h(π) denotes the intermediate hypothesis state at step under the specific permutation π. C. Rubrics for Comparing Generated Hypothesis with Groundtruth Hypothesis Scoring Rubric (04 for each dimension) IMPORTANT INSTRUCTIONS: 1. Score based on RECALL what percentage of GT content is correctly covered by Generated. 2. Both MISSING and WRONG content count as not covered. 3. The examples below are ONLY for illustration do NOT match against them. Score the actual GT vs Generated content, not similarity to examples. Motivation (WHY) Does it identify the same research gap? How to score: Count what percentage of GTs key elements appear correctly in Generated. [EXAMPLE FOR ILLUSTRATION ONLY do not match against this] Example GT: Current deep learning methods for brain tumor segmentation in MRI scans suffer from low accuracy at tumor boundaries, particularly in low-contrast glioma regions, due to insufficient modeling of boundary uncertainty (5 key elements: domain=brain tumor/MRI, task=segmentation, problem=low boundary accuracy, context=low-contrast glioma, cause=insufficient uncertainty modeling) 14 MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier 4 (100%):

sections.title:
Existing DL approaches for brain tumor MRI segmentation have poor boundary delineation in glioma cases
because they fail to model boundary uncertainty
sections.content: All 5: domain + task + problem + context + cause 3 (75%): Missing: Brain tumor segmentation methods have low accuracy at boundaries in gliomas 4: domain + task + problem + context Missing: cause Wrong: Brain tumor segmentation in MRI has low boundary accuracy in gliomas due to limited training data 4: domain + task + problem + context Wrong: cause (limited data vs uncertainty modeling) 2 (50%): Missing: Brain tumor segmentation in MRI has accuracy issues 2.5: domain + task + vague problem Missing: context + cause Wrong: Brain tumor detection in MRI suffers from false positives in gliomas 2: domain + context Wrong: task (detection) + problem (false positives) 1 (25%): Missing: Medical image segmentation needs improvement 1: broad domain only Missing: specific target + problem + context + cause Wrong: Brain tumor classification methods are too slow 1: target organ Wrong: task (classification) + problem (speed) 0 (0%): Protein structure prediction lacks accuracy 0 Completely unrelated domain Natural language models struggle with long-range dependencies 0 Completely unrelated domain Mechanism (HOW IT WORKS) Does it propose the same core mechanism? GT: Apply transformer-based attention with boundary-aware loss functions to learn multi-scale feature representations, enabling precise tumor boundary localization through uncertainty-guided refinement (5 key elements: architecture=transformer attention, loss=boundary-aware, features=multi-scale, task=boundary localization, technique=uncertainty refinement) 4 (100%): Use transformer attention with boundary loss for multi-scale features to localize tumor boundaries via uncertainty-guided refinement All 5: architecture + loss + features + task + technique 15 MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier 3 (75%): Missing: Transformer attention with boundary-aware loss for multi-scale feature learning to localize tumor boundaries 4: architecture + loss + features + task Missing: refinement technique Wrong: Transformer attention with boundary loss for multi-scale features to localize boundaries via post-processing CRF 4: architecture + loss + features + task Wrong: technique (CRF vs uncertainty) 2 (50%): Missing: Use attention mechanism with boundary loss for tumor boundary detection 2.5: partial architecture + loss + task Missing: multi-scale + refinement Wrong: Use transformer attention with standard loss for multi-scale feature learning 2.5: architecture + features Wrong: loss (standard vs boundary) + missing: task + technique 1 (25%): Missing: Apply deep learning for tumor analysis 0.5: broad method category Missing: specific architecture + loss + features + technique Wrong: Use transformer attention for image classification 1: architecture Wrong: task (classification) + missing: loss + features + technique 0 (0%): Apply LSTM for time series forecasting 0 Completely unrelated mechanism and task Use rule-based heuristics for text classification 0 Completely unrelated mechanism and domain Methodology (HOW ITS INTEGRATED) Does it describe similar implementation? GT: Train on BraTS 2021 dataset (1251 MRI cases), implement 3D U-Net with transformer encoder, use combined Dice-boundary loss, apply 5-fold cross-validation, evaluate with Dice score and 95% Hausdorff distance (6 key details: dataset=BraTS 2021/1251, architecture=3D U-Net + transformer, loss=Dice-boundary, validation=5-fold CV, metrics=Dice + HD95) 4 (100%): Train on BraTS 2021 (1251 scans), 3D U-Net with transformer encoder, Dice-boundary loss, 5-fold CV, report Dice and HD95 All 6: dataset + architecture + loss + validation + metrics 3 (75%): Missing: BraTS 2021 dataset, 3D U-Net + transformer, Dice-boundary loss, 5-fold CV, Dice score 5: dataset + architecture + loss + validation + partial metrics Missing: HD 16 MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier Wrong: BraTS 2021 (1251 scans), 3D U-Net + transformer, Dice-boundary loss, 3-fold CV, Dice and HD95 5: dataset + architecture + loss + metrics Wrong: validation (3-fold vs 5-fold) 2 (50%): Missing: Train on BraTS dataset with 3D U-Net, evaluate Dice score 3: partial dataset + architecture + partial metrics Missing: size + loss + validation + HD95 Wrong: Train on private dataset, use 2D U-Net, Dice-boundary loss, 5-fold CV, Dice 3: loss + validation + partial metrics Wrong: dataset + architecture (2D vs 3D) 1 (25%): Missing: Train segmentation model on brain MRI data 1.5: vague dataset + vague approach Missing: specific details Wrong: Train on TCGA genomic data, use ResNet, cross-entropy, accuracy 0.5: general training Wrong: dataset + architecture + loss + metrics 0 (0%): Fine-tune GPT on dialogue dataset with RLHF 0 Completely different domain and methodology Survey 200 patients using questionnaires, analyze with chi-square test 0 Completely different methodology type
